terms.definition: An x-ray scattering technique used to examine nanostructures by measuring the intensity of scattered X-rays at small angles, probing structures from several nanometers to several hundred nanometers.

context_snippets.text: Machine Learning-Assisted Analysis of Small Angle X-ray Scattering Piotr Tomaszewski, Shun Yu, Markus Borg and Jerk R¨onnols RISE Research Institutes of Sweden, Lund and Stockholm, Sweden Email: {piotr.tomaszewski, shun.yu, markus.borg, jerk.ronnols}@ri.se Abstract—Small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) is extensively used in materials science as a way of examining nanostructures.
On our data set, our classiﬁcation models outperform previous work reported in the literature and the accuracy ﬁgures we obtain make them highly relevant to users analyzing SAXS data.

CONCLUSIONS The purpose of this study was to investigate the applicability of ML to assist SAXS analysts in the task of model selection.

terms.definition: An x-ray scattering technique sensitive to the local atomic structure of materials.

context_snippets.text: small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and wide angle X-ray scattering (WAXS).

terms.definition: Materials with designed structures at the nanoscale that can demonstrate unique properties not possible through conventional material design.

context_snippets.text: Richard Feynman [1], efforts to control materials structure have been focused on the nanoscale – this is now widely known as nanomaterials.

terms.definition: SCattering Ai aNalysis, an open-source tool designed to facilitate model selection and classification of SAXS data using machine learning algorithms.

context_snippets.text: As a part of this study, we have produced an open- source scattering analysis tool called SCAN.
In the course of the study, we have developed an open-source tool called SCAN that made it possible for us to create and evaluate eleven ML classiﬁers trained and evaluated on our scattering data.

terms.definition: The geometric arrangement and morphology of materials at the nanometer scale, identified through scattering data analysis.

context_snippets.text: The output is another CSV ﬁle, where each input item is annotated with a predicted nanostructure shape and a corresponding probability (conﬁdence) for each selected ML classiﬁer.

terms.definition: Software used for the simulation and analysis of small-angle scattering data.

terms.definition: A statistical model used in scattering analysis for systems without defined shapes.

context_snippets.text: For statistical models, which are common for systems without deﬁned shapes, three models were selected: Debye-Anderson-Brumberger (DAB) Model [23], Polymer Excluded Volume model [24] and Teubner-Strey model of microemulsions [25].

terms.definition: A statistical model used to simulate scattering data for polymer systems.

Data Generation As aforementioned, ideal scattering intensity is a result of the FT of the electron density distribution inside materials. Thus, SAXS data could be generated via theoretical simulation to meet requirements on large data sets. xray_code_snippets.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import find_peaks, peak_widths, savgol_filter
def detect_1d_peaks(q, intensity, *, prominence=0.05, min_distance=5):
    y = np.asarray(intensity, dtype=float)
    y = np.nan_to_num(y, nan=np.nanmedian(y))
    y_smooth = savgol_filter(y, window_length=11, polyorder=3)
    baseline = np.percentile(y_smooth, 10)
    y_corrected = y_smooth - baseline
    peaks, props = find_peaks(
        y_corrected,
        prominence=prominence * np.nanmax(y_corrected),
        distance=min_distance,
    )
    widths = peak_widths(y_corrected, peaks, rel_height=0.5)
    return [
        {
            "q": float(q[idx]),
            "intensity": float(y_corrected[idx]),
            "prominence": float(props["prominences"][i]),
            "fwhm_samples": float(widths[0][i]),
        }
        for i, idx in enumerate(peaks)
    ]
xray_code_snippets.code_description: Performs 1D peak detection on reduced x-ray scattering data (intensity vs q) using Savitzky-Golay smoothing, baseline correction, and SciPy's find_peaks.
